Marsh

Four days of shingle, smugglers,
marsh churches & ancient gardens

DepartsMon 6 April

The Landscape

Romney Marsh, Dungeness & the Cinque Port towns of Rye and Winchelsea

Dungeness & New Romney — OS 1:16,000
Fairfield Church
Rye — Mermaid Street
Rye — Ypres Tower

“The world, according to the best geographers, is divided into Europe, Asia, Africa, America — and the Romney Marsh.”

Richard Barham, The Ingoldsby Legends, 1837

Romney Marsh is one of England’s great oddities — a vast, flat expanse of reclaimed land jutting into the Channel between Rye and Hythe, most of it below sea level, held back from the sea by centuries of drainage and earthwork. The Romans started it. Medieval monks continued it. The result is a landscape that feels entirely unlike the rest of England: treeless, horizonless, lit by a particular grey and silver light, dotted with isolated Norman churches that seem to have no congregation and no reason to exist where they do. At its tip, Dungeness pushes further into the sea than almost any other point on the south coast — a great triangle of shingle that is classified as the only desert in Britain, home to rare plants, migrant birds, rusted fishing boats, a decommissioned nuclear power station, and the blackened timber cottages of one of the most singular communities in the country.

& Sea Light

Gusbourne Vineyard — Appledore, Kent

Where We're Staying

The House
at Dungeness

A black-clad timber house on the shingle, with vaulted ceilings, wildflower garden, and floor-to-ceiling glass looking out over the desert landscape. One of the most singular places to stay in England.

The wildflower garden in bloom
Morning light across the shingle
Shingle desert beyond the glass
The study — plants, wood and marsh light

“Five and twenty ponies, trotting through the dark — brandy for the Parson, baccy for the Clerk.”

Rudyard Kipling, A Smuggler’s Song, 1906

Rye was, for much of the eighteenth century, effectively controlled by smugglers. The Hawkhurst Gang — at their peak some six hundred men strong — used the Mermaid Inn on Mermaid Street as their headquarters, sitting openly armed while the town looked the other way. They were not a local nuisance but a criminal empire, running brandy, tobacco and tea across the Channel on an industrial scale and murdering anyone who threatened to expose them. Their most audacious act was the 1747 raid on the Poole Customs House, breaking in to reclaim a seized cargo of tea, then hunting down and killing the witnesses. Their leader was eventually gibbeted. The gibbet cage — and the skull inside it — is still in the attic of the Ypres Tower, which you can visit for £10. Kipling wrote his “Smuggler’s Song” from just down the coast at Burwash. The last line has been over every smuggling pub door in Kent ever since: “Watch the wall, my darling, while the Gentlemen go by.”

The Four
Days

Two protected sketching mornings, five marsh churches, the Rye–Winchelsea circular walk, and Great Dixter in April bloom. Fair and poor weather options throughout.

Day One

Friday 3 April
Arrive & Absorb

  • 12–1pm

    🍷 Vineyard Picnic Lunch

    Gusbourne Vineyard, Kenardington Rd, Appledore TN26 2BE. One of England's finest sparkling wine estates.

  • 1pm

    Check in — Sunspot

    New Romney TN29 9NF. Drop bags. Resist filling the afternoon.

  • 2:30

    RSPB Dungeness — Afternoon Walk

    20-min walk across the shingle from Sunspot. April migrants arriving — marsh harriers, warblers, avocets. Follow the reserve paths at your own pace; hides open all day.

  • 5pm

    Dungeness Beach & Boat Wrecks

    Walk back along the shingle past the fishing huts and wrecked boats as the light drops. The landscape at dusk is extraordinary.

  • 6–7pm

    Supper — The Pilot Inn

    Legendary fish & chips. Book ahead.

Day Two

Saturday 4 April
Sketch & Churches

  • 8–9am

    Breakfast

  • 9:15am

    Drive to Ivychurch

    15 minutes across the marsh.

  • 9:30am

    The Church Walk — 3.5 miles

    2.5–3 hrs · Good boots recommended

    The most historically dense morning on the marsh. Three medieval churches strung together across flat drover paths, finishing at the pub exactly at opening time.

  • ▸ St George's, Ivychurch

    133ft long — the Cathedral of Romney Marsh. Built under the Archbishop of Canterbury, far larger than any congregation it ever served. Smugglers allegedly stored contraband here; tunnels said to connect it to the Bell Inn next door.

  • ▸ St Eanswith's, Brenzett

    One of the smallest churches on the marsh. Village in the Domesday Book, 1086. The Fagge Monument here inspired E. Nesbit's ghost story Man-Size in Marble.

  • ▸ Fairfield — St Thomas Becket

    Unmissable. Isolated in a field of sheep, reached on foot across a raised causeway. Collect key from Becket House gatepost. Return via Snargate.

  • 12:30pm

    Lunch — The Bell Inn, Ivychurch

    Booked. CAMRA award-winning community pub, dog-friendly, beer garden overlooked by St George's tower. Fish and chips highly rated.

  • Optional

    St Clement's, Old Romney

    Interior painted pink for Disney's Dr Syn: The Scarecrow of Romney Marsh. Derek Jarman buried here — a connection back to Sunspot.

  • 2–5:30pm

    Chill — House time

  • 5:30pm

    Supper — Chilli Con Carne

  • 6pm

    🚗 Dad drives to Heathrow

    Pick up Blake.

  • 7–10pm

    Movie night

  • 10pm

    Dad returns with Blake 🎉

Day Three

Sunday 5 April
Rye & Smugglers

  • 8–9am

    Breakfast

  • 9am

    🐣 Easter Egg Hunt

    Easter Sunday

    One hour on the shingle around the house. Plenty of hiding spots among the sea kale and driftwood.

  • 10:30

    Rye — Sketching Morning

    Fair: Gun GardenFoul: cobbles & bakeries
  • 1–2pm

    Lunch — Rye

  • 2pm

    Rye Heritage Centre & Ypres Tower

    Sound-and-light Town Model £4.50. Gibbet cage in the secret attic.

  • 4:30

    Winchelsea — St Thomas's

    Fair weather only

    Spike Milligan's grave. Sailors' graffiti on pillars.

  • 4:45pm

    🍷 Early Supper — The Globe Inn

    Rye. One of the best pubs on the marsh — book ahead for Easter Sunday.

Day Four

Monday 6 April
Walk & Dixter

  • 8–9am

    Breakfast

  • 9:30

    House time & Pack up

  • 11am

    🚗 Check out — Sunspot

    TN29 9NF. Board the Romney, Hythe & Dymchurch Railway.

  • 11am

    🚜 RH&DR Railway to Hythe

    World-famous 15-inch gauge steam railway. Ride to the end of the line.

  • 12:30pm

    Lunch — The White Hart, Hythe

    Booked.

  • 2:15pm

    🚜 Return train to Dungeness

  • 3:30pm

    🚗 Transfer to Great Dixter

  • 4:15pm

    Great Dixter House & Gardens

    45 mins

    April tulips, blossom and the famous long border waking up. Closes 5pm.

Food & Drink

Where to Eat

Friday & Saturday evenings
The Pilot Inn
🏭 Battery Road, Dungeness TN29 9NJ

Family-run beachfront pub-restaurant in the heart of Dungeness National Nature Reserve. Widely regarded as serving the best fish and chips in Kent. Extraordinary interior, dog-friendly, local ales. Mon–Sat noon–8pm, Sun noon–7pm.

thepilotdungeness.co.uk ↗
Saturday lunch
The Bell Inn
🏭 Ivychurch, Romney Marsh

CAMRA award-winning community pub next to St George's Church, Ivychurch — allegedly connected by smugglers' tunnels. Large beer garden overlooked by the church tower. Fish and chips and roasts come highly rated. Dog-friendly. Booked 12:30pm.

thebellinnivychurch.com ↗
Sunday evening
The Globe Inn
🏭 Rye, East Sussex

One of the best pubs on the marsh — a proper Rye local with log fires, good food and a relaxed atmosphere. Ideal for an early Easter Sunday supper after Winchelsea. Booked 4:45pm.

ramblinns.com ↗
Monday lunch
The White Hart
🏭 Hythe, Kent

Passionate about good food — a well-regarded pub-restaurant in the centre of Hythe, dog-friendly, with a strong seasonal menu. Perfectly placed after the RH&DR steam railway ride from Dungeness. Booked 12:30pm.

thewhiteharthythe.com ↗

Great Dixter
Monday 6 April

Northiam, East Sussex · Opens 11am · Allow 2.5 hrs

Further Reading

Useful Links

History & Villages Romney Marsh History — Villages Guide Detailed histories of every village on the marsh — smuggling lore, church records, local legends and the stories behind the place names. romneymarshhistory.co.uk/villages Walking Guide Romney Marsh Medieval Churches Walk A comprehensive walking guide linking the marsh churches together — routes, distances, terrain notes and what to look for inside each building. walkingclub.org.uk Viewpoint Guide Fairfield Church — Discovering Britain The Discovering Britain guide to Fairfield St Thomas Becket — history, access notes, and why this isolated building in a field of sheep is one of England's most remarkable sights. discoveringbritain.org Rye Museum Smuggling in Rye & Romney Marsh The Rye Museum's account of the Hawkhurst Gang, the trade routes, the violence, and the extraordinary hold the smuggling networks had over this stretch of coast in the 18th century. ryemuseum.co.uk
